title: Mortgage Costs Jump After War Starts
summary: A new report shows that getting a mortgage now costs much more than before a war began. For a typical home loan of £250,000 over 25 years, the yearly cost has gone up by £788. This is because banks have raised interest rates and removed some cheaper deals since the US and Israel started strikes on Iran in late February. The average two-year fixed rate is now 5.28%, up from 4.83% in early March. For a five-year deal, it's £651 more expensive than two weeks ago. Experts say borrowers should plan ahead as rates might change more due to global events. First-time buyers might find it harder to get affordable loans.

an interest rate that stays the same for a set time

background_read: A mortgage is a loan people use to buy a house. They pay back the loan over many years with extra money called interest. Interest rates can go up or down based on things like wars or the economy. When rates go up, mortgages become more expensive. This article talks about how a recent war has made mortgages cost more, especially for first-time buyers.
Article_Structure: WHO: Homeowners, buyers, banks like Barclays and HSBC, and experts like Adam French.
WHAT: Mortgage costs have increased by £788 per year for a typical loan due to higher interest rates.
WHERE: In the UK, as reported by BBC News.
WHY: Because banks raised rates after the US and Israel started strikes on Iran, causing uncertainty.

perspectives.description: Borrowers are worried about higher costs and need to plan for changes when their current deals end.
